items.question: If you heat ice, it ___.
explanations: The zero conditional uses the present simple, not 'will', for a general truth.
Correct! A general truth takes the present simple in both clauses: 'it melts'.
'Melted' is past simple, but this is always true.
'Is melting' is present continuous (now), not a general fact.

items.question: If I ___ tired, I go to bed early.
explanations: Correct! The if-clause uses the present simple: 'If I am tired'.
'Will be' can't go in the if-clause of a zero conditional.
'Was' is past simple, but this is a general habit.
'Would be' belongs to the second conditional, not this general truth.

items.question: Water ___ if you cool it to 0°C.
explanations: A scientific fact takes the present simple, not 'will'.
Correct! A general truth uses the present simple: 'Water freezes'.
'Froze' is past simple, but this is always true.
'Freezing' needs an auxiliary and isn't the right form here.

items.question: If the traffic light ___ red, cars stop.
explanations: Correct! The if-clause uses the present simple: 'If the light is red'.
'Will be' can't follow 'if' in a zero conditional.
'Was' is past simple, but this is a general rule.
'Be' is the base form; it must agree: 'is'.

items.question: If you ___ this button, the machine starts.
explanations: 'Will press' can't go in the if-clause of a zero conditional.
Correct! The condition uses the present simple: 'If you press'.
'Pressed' is past simple, but this is a general instruction.
'Pressing' isn't a full verb here.

items.question: If people don't sleep, they ___ tired.
explanations: As a general truth, the result takes the present simple, not 'will'.
Correct! A general truth uses the present simple: 'they feel tired'.
'Felt' is past simple, but this is always true.
'Are feeling' is present continuous, not a general fact.

items.question: If the alarm ___, everyone leaves the building.
explanations: Correct! The if-clause of a rule takes the present simple: 'If the alarm rings'.
'Will ring' can't follow 'if' in a zero conditional.
'Rang' is past simple, but this is a standing rule.
'Ringing' isn't a full verb here.

items.question: ___ you touch fire, you get burned.
explanations: Correct! In the zero conditional, 'when' and 'if' are almost the same: 'When you touch fire, you get burned'.
'Will' isn't a linker to start this clause.
'Did' would make it a past question, not a general truth.
'Would' belongs to the second conditional.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'When you touch fire, you get burned.'
options: If you touch fire, you get burned.
If you touch fire, you will get burned.
If you touched fire, you got burned.
You get burned when you will touch fire.
explanations: Correct! For a general truth, 'when' = 'if', both with the present simple.
'Will get burned' turns it into a first conditional about a specific future.
The past tenses describe one past event, not a general truth.
'When you will touch' is wrong — no 'will' after when/if here.

items.question: Correct this: 'If you will mix red and white, you get pink.'
options: If you mix red and white, you get pink.
If you mixed red and white, you got pink.
If you will mix red and white, you will get pink.
When you will mix red and white, you get pink.
explanations: Correct! Both clauses of a zero conditional use the present simple: 'If you mix… you get…'.
The past tenses describe one past event, not a general truth.
The zero conditional never uses 'will' — this describes a fact.
'When you will mix' is wrong — no 'will' after when/if here.
